annotations.label: Compact operator (normed spaces)

anchor.snippet: is called compact if it maps bounded subsets of

annotations.note: `IsCompactOperator f := ∃ K, IsCompact K ∧ f ⁻¹' K ∈ 𝓝 0` is Mathlib's compact operator predicate, equivalent to the Wikipedia definition on normed spaces.

annotations.label: Sequential characterization of compactness

anchor.snippet: is compact if the image of the closed unit ball of

annotations.note: This iff characterization via the closed unit ball (and its `ball` sibling) is exactly the Wikipedia sequential characterization.

annotations.label: Compact implies bounded (normed)

anchor.snippet: Every compact linear operator between normed spaces is bounded

annotations.note: For a semilinear map, `IsCompactOperator ⇒ Continuous` (i.e. bounded) is proved directly.

annotations.label: Totally bounded characterization into Banach spaces

anchor.snippet: compactness may also be expressed by saying that the image of every bounded subset

annotations.note: The image of any bounded set has compact (hence totally bounded) closure; when the codomain is Banach this coincides with the Wikipedia total-boundedness statement.

annotations.label: Compact operator (TVS)

anchor.snippet: The following statements are equivalent, and different authors may pick any one of these as the principal definition

annotations.note: `IsCompactOperator` is defined for arbitrary topological vector spaces via the neighborhood-of-zero characterization, with `isCompactOperator_iff_exists_mem_nhds_image_subset_compact` giving the image-of-neighborhood form.

annotations.label: Finite-rank operators are compact

anchor.snippet: Every finite-rank operator is compact.

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `FiniteRank` predicate for operators, but any continuous linear map into a locally compact (e.g. finite-dimensional) space is compact, which subsumes the finite-rank case.

annotations.label: Identity compact iff finite-dimensional

anchor.snippet: is finite-dimensional if and only if its identity operator

annotations.note: Verbatim iff over a nontrivially normed field with `CompleteSpace 𝕜`.

annotations.label: Invertible compact operator forces finite-dimensional

anchor.snippet: More generally, if a Banach space

annotations.note: Mathlib proves that if the identity is compact then the space is finite-dimensional, from which the invertible-compact-operator corollary is immediate, but the corollary itself is not stated as a lemma.

annotations.label: Compact implies bounded and continuous

anchor.snippet: Every compact linear operator between normed spaces is bounded, and hence continuous.

annotations.note: Same lemma as (fd5db4553b4c): a compact linear map is automatically continuous.

annotations.label: Compact operators form a closed subspace

anchor.snippet: is a closed linear subspace of

annotations.note: Together with the submodule `compactOperator` (add/smul/zero closure), this gives the closed-subspace structure of K(X,Y) in L(X,Y).

annotations.label: Compact operators are a two-sided ideal

anchor.snippet: Compact operators are stable under composition with bounded operators.

annotations.note: Both `IsCompactOperator.comp_clm` (precompose) and `IsCompactOperator.clm_comp` (postcompose) with a CLM produce a compact operator — the two-sided-ideal property.

annotations.label: Calkin algebra and essential spectrum

anchor.snippet: the ring

annotations.note: No Calkin algebra or essential-spectrum definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Approximation by finite-rank on Hilbert spaces

anchor.snippet: every compact operator between Hilbert spaces is the operator-norm limit of finite-rank operators

annotations.note: Mathlib has no finite-rank operator concept and no approximation-by-finite-rank theorem for Hilbert-space compact operators.

annotations.label: Enflo counter-example to approximation property

anchor.snippet: in 1973 Per Enflo gave a counter-example

annotations.note: The approximation property and Enflo's counter-example are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Schauder's theorem

anchor.snippet: this result is known as Schauder

annotations.note: Grep for `IsCompactOperator.*adjoint` returns nothing; Schauder's theorem (T compact iff T* compact) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Range of a compact operator is separable

anchor.snippet: is compact, then the closure of the range of

annotations.note: No `IsCompactOperator` lemma about separability of the range closure exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Compact implies strictly singular

anchor.snippet: Every compact operator between Banach spaces is strictly singular

annotations.note: Strictly singular operators are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: I − T is Fredholm of index zero

anchor.snippet: is a Fredholm operator of index zero

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Fredholm-operator definition or index; a TODO in `Banach.lean` explicitly notes this gap.

annotations.label: Fredholm alternative

anchor.snippet: has a unique solution

annotations.note: The Fredholm alternative is stated exactly: for a compact operator T and μ ≠ 0, either μ is an eigenvalue of T or μ lies in the resolvent set.

annotations.label: Parameter-dependent Fredholm alternative

anchor.snippet: Failure of invertibility occurs precisely when

annotations.note: `hasEigenvalue_iff_mem_spectrum` says that for μ ≠ 0, μ is in the spectrum of a compact operator iff it is an eigenvalue — the parameter-dependent alternative.

annotations.label: 0 belongs to the spectrum of a compact operator

anchor.snippet: belongs to the spectrum

annotations.note: The specific fact that 0 is in the spectrum of any compact operator on an infinite-dimensional space is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spectrum of a compact operator

anchor.snippet: Apart from

annotations.note: This lemma states exactly that the nonzero spectrum of a compact operator consists of its (nonzero) eigenvalues.

annotations.label: Nonzero spectrum is finite or countable with 0 as only accumulation

anchor.snippet: The nonzero spectrum is therefore either finite or countably infinite.

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma states the finite-or-countable spectrum with 0-accumulation property for compact operators.

annotations.label: Equal kernel and cokernel dimensions

anchor.snippet: has finite-dimensional kernel and cokernel, and these dimensions are equal

annotations.note: Depends on Fredholm-index theory, which is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Adjoint shares nonzero spectrum

anchor.snippet: The adjoint has the same nonzero spectral values.

annotations.note: No `IsCompactOperator` lemma relating the spectra of T and its adjoint exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Operator with compact resolvent

anchor.snippet: is said to have compact resolvent if

annotations.note: The notion of an unbounded operator with compact resolvent is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spectral theorem for compact self-adjoint operators

anchor.snippet: then every nonzero element of the spectrum of

annotations.note: The eigenspaces of a compact self-adjoint operator span the ambient Hilbert space; combined with `finite_dimensional_eigenspace` and `hasEigenvalue_iff_mem_spectrum`, this is the compact-self-adjoint spectral theorem.

annotations.label: Diagonal representation of compact self-adjoint operator

anchor.snippet: decomposes as an orthogonal direct sum of the eigenspaces

annotations.note: The proof that eigenspaces span the whole Hilbert space is present, but the explicit orthogonal-direct-sum decomposition (an `IsHilbertSum` statement) for a compact self-adjoint operator is not.

annotations.label: Extension to compact normal operators

anchor.snippet: The same description applies, with complex eigenvalues, to compact normal operators

annotations.note: No analogous spectral decomposition for compact normal (non-self-adjoint) operators is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Singular-value decomposition of compact operators

anchor.snippet: Nevertheless, it has a singular-value decomposition.

annotations.note: Mathlib defines singular values only for maps between finite-dimensional inner-product spaces; SVD for general compact operators (the infinite-dimensional expansion) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Hilbert–Schmidt and trace-class operators

anchor.snippet: is Hilbert–Schmidt when

annotations.note: Hilbert–Schmidt and trace-class operators are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trace-class ⊂ Hilbert–Schmidt ⊂ compact

anchor.snippet: Every trace-class operator is Hilbert–Schmidt, and every Hilbert–Schmidt operator is compact.

annotations.note: Neither the trace-class nor the Hilbert–Schmidt operator ideal is present, so this inclusion chain is not formalized.

annotations.label: Completely continuous operator

anchor.snippet: is called completely continuous if, for every weakly convergent sequence

annotations.note: The completely-continuous-operator predicate (weak→norm sequential continuity) is not defined.

annotations.label: Compact ⇒ completely continuous; converse on reflexive spaces

anchor.snippet: Compact operators between Banach spaces are always completely continuous

annotations.note: No comparison between compact and completely continuous operators is stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite-rank operators

anchor.snippet: Finite-rank operators are the simplest examples of compact operators.

annotations.note: No `FiniteRank` predicate exists, but any CLM into a locally compact (finite-dim) space is compact, which captures the finite-rank example.

annotations.label: Diagonal/multiplication operators on sequence spaces

anchor.snippet: One class of examples is given by diagonal or multiplication operators on sequence spaces.

annotations.note: Multiplication/diagonal operators on ℓᵖ with compactness characterization are not formalized.

annotations.label: Integral operators with regular kernel

anchor.snippet: Integral operators also provide compact operators in many important cases.

annotations.note: No general integral-kernel operator with a compactness theorem is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hilbert–Schmidt integral operator on L²

anchor.snippet: On Hilbert spaces, Hilbert–Schmidt integral operators are compact

annotations.note: Hilbert–Schmidt integral operators are not defined in Mathlib, and no compactness lemma for them exists.

annotations.label: Identity on infinite-dimensional Banach space is not compact

anchor.snippet: The identity operator on an infinite-dimensional Banach space is a basic non-example.

annotations.note: The identity of an infinite-dimensional Banach space fails to be compact by the contrapositive of `isCompactOperator_id_iff_finiteDimensional`.

annotations.label: Shift operators are not compact

anchor.snippet: The unilateral shift operators on sequence spaces are also not compact.

annotations.note: Unilateral shift operators on ℓᵖ are not treated as a named example in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bounded operators into nuclear spaces are compact

anchor.snippet: Every bounded linear operator from a normed space into a complete nuclear space is compact

annotations.note: Nuclear spaces are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cauchy integral operator

anchor.snippet: A complex-analytic example is given by the Cauchy integral operator

annotations.note: The Cauchy integral operator as a specific compact-operator example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Compact embedding

anchor.snippet: The embedding is called compact if this inclusion map is a compact operator

annotations.note: No `CompactEmbedding` concept exists; one would have to state `IsCompactOperator (inclusion : X → Y)` by hand.

annotations.label: Rellich–Kondrachov theorem

anchor.snippet: The Rellich–Kondrachov theorem yields other compact embeddings.

annotations.note: No Rellich–Kondrachov theorem is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lebesgue space inclusion is not compact

anchor.snippet: A non-example is the inclusion of Lebesgue spaces

annotations.note: The failure of compactness for Lᵖ ↪ Lq inclusions is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hardy space embeds compactly into holomorphic functions

anchor.snippet: Holomorphic function spaces provide another class of compact embeddings.

annotations.note: Hardy spaces are not defined in Mathlib, so this compact-embedding example cannot be stated.

annotations.label: Bergman space compact embedding

anchor.snippet: The same principle applies to Bergman spaces

annotations.note: Bergman spaces are not defined in Mathlib.
